text: The surgeon begins the sigmoid resection by suspending the uterus to create space, examining the extent of diverticular disease and associated stricture. The proximal extent is marked, and the medial to lateral dissection is initiated with a higher ligation of the mesentery for better reach. The superior hemorrhoidal artery is isolated distal to the left colic artery, visible in the surgical field. The medial dissection progresses, followed by a limited lateral dissection to meet the medial plane.

The surgeon identifies thick adhesions to the pelvic sidewall, causing angulation of the bowel at the rectosigmoid junction. The adhesions are carefully dissected to reveal the intrinsic stricture. The mobilization is completed, and the distal margin is selected for transvaginal extraction. The colon is prepared by preserving the left colic artery up to the marked proximal margin.

The surgeon positions the stapler in place and administers ICG to visualize the margin before firing the stapler to divide the distal bowel. The stapler port is placed through the right rectus for reduced patient pain, altering the camera view to arm number two in the epigastrium. The surgeon performs a colpotomy near the cervix, avoiding the rectovaginal septum, and extracts the specimen without a bag. The colpotomy is closed using a 2-0, twelve-inch V-lock suture, focusing on securing mucosal and muscular wall bites to address bleeding from torn mucosal edges.

The surgeon focuses on closing the muscular wall and mucosa, avoiding the overlying peritoneum. The camera is repositioned for a better view of the closure process. The surgeon performs a side-to-side, end-to-end anastomosis by joining the two posterior staple lines, followed by creating a colotomy and proctotomy. An inner layer is sewn, followed by an outer layer. Indocyanine green (ICG) is used to check perfusion, showing enhanced perfusion of the mucosa and serosa, particularly on the rectal side.

The surgeon begins the anastomosis by sewing from the middle to the right, then over the top, tying the sutures together. A rigid scope is used to perform a leak test through the anastomosis, confirming no bubbles and a widely patent connection.
